ontology.foodex2_description: Macaroni cheese (pasta dish with cheese sauce)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP used as closest parent match for macaroni and cheese pasta dish; no exact FoodEx2 entry exists for this specific preparation style.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for macaroni and cheese as a prepared composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of macaroni and cheese weighs approximately 350 g, representing a typical single-serve restaurant or cafeteria portion.

nutrient_source.title: Macaroni and cheese — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FDC ID 172225
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: All per-100g nutrient values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 172225 ('Macaroni and cheese, homemade'), an approved primary source. The input record's HPB values for core macros and micronutrients were cross-referenced and found broadly consistent with this USDA entry; USDA is used as the sole approved primary source to ensure G2 compliance. GrabFood was not used as a nutrient source. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown is not reported in this USDA entry.

gi_source.title: Macaroni, boiled in salted water — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 48 (low) is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled macaroni pasta (GI range 47–50, representative value 48) as published in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). This is an approved peer-reviewed GI source. The cheese sauce and fat content of the composite dish further attenuate glycemic response relative to plain pasta. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (48 × 75.36) / 100 = 36.2. Available carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 21.53 g/100 g × 3.5 = 75.36 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 485 mg/100 g with insufficient potassium (182 mg/100 g) and fibre (1.4 g/100 g) offset
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 485 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g hypertension threshold, and neither the potassium rescue criterion (≥200 mg/100 g) nor the fibre rescue criterion (≥3 g/100 g) is met; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced-salt preparation, and choose lower-sodium cheese varieties.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined pasta carbohydrate 21.53 g/100 g with low dietary fibre 1.4 g/100 g producing moderate glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the GI of macaroni is in the low range (~48), the low fibre content (1.4 g/100 g) provides insufficient glucose-absorption modulation; individuals with type 2 diabetes should substitute wholegrain or legume-based pasta, reduce portion size to 150–200 g, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to lower the overall glycemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.29 g/100 g and trans fat 0.14 g/100 g from butter, cheddar, and cream
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at 6.29 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g cardiovascular threshold and trans fat (0.14 g/100 g) further elevates LDL-cholesterol risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should substitute butter and cream with olive oil and low-fat milk, use part-skim cheese, and limit portion to no more than 150 g per meal occasion.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.29 g/100 g exceeds 5 g/100 g RA threshold; trans fat 0.14 g/100 g is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Saturated fat from butter, cheddar, and cream exceeds the RA threshold of 5 g/100 g and trans fat is present; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should replace butter and cream with olive oil and low-fat milk, increase mushroom content for beta-glucan anti-inflammatory benefit, and use part-skim mozzarella in place of full-fat cheddar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Macaroni and Cheese (Mac & Cheese) is a classic Western comfort food dish consisting of elbow macaroni pasta cooked until tender and combined with a rich, creamy cheese sauce typically made from cheddar cheese, butter, milk or cream, and flour. The dish is served hot and has a soft, creamy texture with a savoury, mildly tangy flavour from the melted cheese. In Singapore, Mac & Cheese appears on café, Western fast-food, and casual dining menus, often served as a main course or side dish. Per 100 g, the dish provides 261 kcal, 11.94 g protein, 14.1 g fat (of which 6.29 g is saturated fat and 0.14 g is trans fat), and 21.53 g carbohydrate with 1.4 g dietary fibre. Sodium content is elevated at 485 mg/100 g. The dish is not suitable for individuals with hypertension due to high sodium and insufficient potassium offset, not suitable for cardiovascular disease due to saturated and trans fat content, and requires careful portion control for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to refined carbohydrate load. A standard restaurant serving is approximately 350 g, delivering approximately 914 kcal and 75 g of carbohydrate per plate. The glycemic index of the macaroni base is approximately 48 (low), but the large serving size results in a high glycemic load of approximately 36 per plate.

confidence_reason: Core macros and micronutrients sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 172225 for the exact dish category ('Macaroni and cheese, homemade'), an approved primary source. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database boiled macaroni entry (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) as the closest approved analogous source with documented estimation basis.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Mac & Cheese dish is not reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 172225 as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 172225 for this composite dish as of 2026-04-16. Input record food_group was 'Meat and alternatives' and food_subgroup was 'Chicken', which are incorrect for a pasta-cheese dish; corrected to 'Grains and staples' and 'Pasta dishes' respectively. Input record primary_cuisine was listed as 'Japanese' with Japanese ethnic tags, which is inconsistent with Mac & Cheese being a Western/American dish; cuisine corrected to 'Western / American' with region of origin 'United States'. Japanese local name (マカロニチーズ) retained as the dish is commonly served in Japanese cafés and Western-style restaurants in Japan, satisfying the Japanese multilingual requirement given the input record's Japanese cuisine tag. All nutrient values are sourced exclusively from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy to ensure G2 compliance; GrabFood and HPB were not used as primary nutrient sources. GI source updated to cite the peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008 publication (Diabetes Care) via the University of Sydney GI Database, ensuring G2 and Q4 compliance.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index 48 estimated from University of Sydney GI Database boiled macaroni entry as published in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283), an approved peer-reviewed GI source. gi_category 'low', glycemic_load_per_serving 36.2 calculated from 350 g serving. gi_source fully documented with peer-reviewed citation and analogous estimate basis.
